Those who profess faith in Christ as the Lord of glory must not show favoritism based on outward circumstances and appearances, a practice inconsistent with being disciples of the lowly Jesus. James does not here encourage rudeness or disorder; civil respect is due; but never to the extent that it influences decisions within the church of Christ, such as assigning offices or imposing discipline, or in any matter of religion. Examining ourselves is beneficial in every aspect of our spiritual lives. Let us do this often and use every opportunity to reflect on our souls. Since places of worship require expense, those who contribute should be accommodated; however, if people were more spiritually minded, the poor would receive greater attention in worshipping congregations. A humble state fosters inner peace and growth in holiness. God would give all believers riches and honors of this world if these things would benefit them, since he has chosen them to be rich in faith and made them heirs of his kingdom, which he promised to bestow on all who love him. Consider how often riches lead to vice and harm, and how people of wealth, power, and worldly status reproach God and religion; it will reveal this sin as very sinful and foolish. The Scripture commands us to love our neighbor as ourselves. This is a royal law, coming from the King of kings; and if Christians act unjustly, they are convicted by the law as transgressors. To think that our good deeds can atone for our bad deeds plainly leads us to seek another atonement. According to the covenant of works, one violation of any command brings condemnation, from which no past, present, or future obedience can deliver a person. This demonstrates the happiness of those who are in Christ. We may serve him without slavish fear. God’s restrictions are not bondage, but our own corruptions are. The judgment passed upon impenitent sinners will be condemnation without mercy. But God finds joy and glory in pardoning and blessing those who might justly be condemned at his tribunal; and his grace teaches those who experience his mercy to reflect it in their actions.

Those are mistaken who consider a mere intellectual belief of the gospel as equivalent to true evangelical religion, as many do now. No doubt, genuine faith alone, by which people share in Christ’s righteousness, atonement, and grace, saves their souls; but it produces good works, demonstrating its reality through its effect on actions; while mere agreement with a doctrine or historical belief of facts is entirely different from this saving faith. A superficial profession may gain the approval of devout people and procure worldly advantages; but what profit would it be for anyone to gain the whole world and lose their soul? Can such faith save someone? Everything should be considered profitable or unprofitable based on whether it advances or hinders our salvation. This passage clearly shows that belief, or agreement with the gospel, without works is not faith. There is no way to demonstrate that we truly believe in Christ except by being diligent in good works, motivated by and for the purpose of the gospel. People may boast to others and be proud of something they do not possess. True faith involves not only agreement but consent; not only belief in the truth of the word but acceptance of Christ. Genuine believing is not merely an act of understanding but a work of the whole heart. The fact that justifying faith cannot exist without works is shown by the examples of Abraham and Rahab. Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness. Faith producing such works advanced him to special favor. We see then how that by works a person is justified, not by a mere profession or belief without obedience, but by having faith that produces good works. And being willing to deny one’s own reason, affections, and interests is an action that tests a believer. Observe here the amazing power of faith in transforming sinners. Rahab's actions proved her faith was alive, demonstrating that she believed with her heart, not merely through intellectual assent. Let us be careful, for even the best works without faith are dead; they lack root and principle. By faith, everything we do is truly good, done in obedience to God and aiming at his acceptance; otherwise, it is as though the root were dead, lacking fruit. Faith is the root, good works are the fruits; and we must ensure that we have both. This is the grace of God in which we stand, and we should remain steadfast in it. There is no middle ground; everyone must either be a friend of God or an enemy.
